# Alex Chen — VP of Platform Engineering

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Role | VP of Platform Engineering |
| Name | Alex Chen (hypothetical) |
| Scope | Platform Infrastructure, Developer Tools, API Services |
| Reports to | CTO |
| Direct reports | 4 engineering managers, 1 staff architect |
| Team size | ~30 engineers across 4 squads |

## Squads in Scope

| Squad | Focus Area |
|-------|-----------|
| Platform Core | Infrastructure primitives, CI/CD, runtime environments |
| API Services | Public and internal APIs, gateway, rate limiting |
| Developer Experience | CLI tools, SDKs, onboarding, documentation |
| Security Engineering | Auth, secrets management, vulnerability remediation |

## Strategic Goals (Current Quarter)

1. **Ship the v3 API migration** — Move all public endpoints to v3 before
   end of quarter. Success = 80% of external traffic on v3.
2. **Reduce developer onboarding time** — New engineers should be able to
   ship their first PR within 48 hours of access. Currently at ~5 days.
3. **Zero critical vulnerabilities in production** — All CVEs rated critical
   must be patched within 72 hours of disclosure.
4. **Platform reliability** — Maintain 99.95% uptime on core platform
   services. No more than 2 incidents per month rated SEV-2 or higher.
5. **Automation over toil** — Every recurring manual process should have a
   plan to automate. Target: eliminate 3 manual workflows this quarter.

## Kudos — What to Celebrate

Alex values recognition that is **specific and public**.

- A launch shipped on time or ahead of schedule
- A team member went above and beyond (mentioned in comments)
- A difficult technical problem was solved creatively
- An initiative completed that directly advances a quarterly goal
- A process improvement saved measurable time or reduced incidents
- A compliance review passed on the first attempt (rare and worth noting)
- Cross-squad collaboration produced a better outcome than either squad alone

## Feedback — What to Course-Correct

Alex wants early signal, not full-blown escalation. He prefers to ask
questions in 1:1s rather than issue directives.

- A launch is trending behind schedule but not yet blocked
- Scope is creeping without explicit acknowledgment
- A decision was made that conflicts with a quarterly goal
- Quality signals are declining (increasing bug reports, test failures)
- A team is working in isolation when the work affects other squads
- Documentation or communication is lagging behind shipping
- An approach was chosen that adds tech debt without a paydown plan

## Pre-Escalation — When to Get Involved

Alex wants to intervene early when a conversation, decision, or resource
shift can prevent a bigger problem. He can escalate to the CTO.

- A launch has a blocker that the team can't resolve at their level
- Two squads have conflicting priorities and no resolution path
- A dependency on another org is unresponsive (2+ attempts, no reply)
- A security vulnerability is approaching the 72-hour SLA
- A key person is overloaded across multiple critical-path launches
- A compliance review is overdue and blocking a Beta/GA transition
- Stakeholder expectations are misaligned with what's being built
- A target date is at risk and the team hasn't communicated the slip

## Weekly Rhythm

1. **Monday morning** — Scan the brief. Star items for follow-up.
2. **Monday 1:1s** — Open with Kudos. Frame Feedback as coaching questions.
   Ask "what do you need from me?" on Pre-Escalation items.
3. **Tuesday staff meeting** — Share Kudos broadly. Discuss Pre-Escalation
   items needing cross-squad coordination.
